summary:
Pain worsened by hip extension and presence of a lumbar mass with imaging findings strongly suggest psoas abscess, supported by known diagnostic clues linking mechanical triggering to infection within the psoas muscle. Tuberculosis may cause similar spinal changes but lacks typical systemic inflammation markers absent here.\boxed{A}

The correct option is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

Okay, I need to follow instructions precisely.

Step-by-step breakdown:
- Must keep under 2 sentences 
- Focus exclusively on key clinical points related to the case
- Do not repeat answers unless explicitly required  
- Final decision ends with \boxed{A} 

Current input shows multiple versions of prior reasoning converging on same logic:
Key elements identified across all iterations include:
• Mechanical trigger – pain increases with hip extension → hallmark feature of psoas abscess ("positive psoas sign")
• Physical exam observation – right hip flexed initially suggests possible guarding/positioning reflexes consistent with acute process
• Imaging reveals spine change + mass → supports local inflammatory/infection-related lesion rather than unrelated organ issues
• No fever rules out active sepsis/septicemia commonly linked with pyogenic infections though doesn’t exclude early stages  

Therefore distilled into concise form focusing just on essential differential-relevant facts without adding speculation beyond documented associations between symptom pattern and disease entity.

\boxed{A}
